topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?
text: We should not use ChatGPT for homework because it has been proven that it has led to greater reliance on the AI at the expense of critical thinking skills of students. Students might outsource their thinking instead of having to learn and process information for themselves that is usually necessary in order to complete homework. This is foundational as a learning step and almost all recognized teaching pedagogies acknowledge the importance of thinking for oneself and thinking through problem

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?
text: But in the age of AI, embracing AI and preparing students for the working world with AI is just as important. The critical aspect about using AI is the prompt and generating answers through "intelligent" prompt will help the students through their critical thinking process. How to craft out appropriate prompts are important. It’s like a critical part od learning too! To ask intelligent questions.

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?
text: Sure, but not all students might be aware or even know how to intelligently use such prompts. topicQuestion: Go for higher pay at expense of social life (For) or take lower pay to enjoy life (Against)?
text: A higher pay enables a higher income. A higher income is an enabler for many factors in a high quality of living. These include providing good healthcare for your family, being able to provide for children’s education needs, freedom to travel to places you want to and live a lifestyle that you want. Further, less social life doesn’t mean no social life. It is very possible to have an enriching social life with a tighter circle of friends, even with less time overall.

topicQuestion: Go for higher pay at expense of social life (For) or take lower pay to enjoy life (Against)?
text: I can think of families where your argument would make sense, especially where there that higher income could help meet some basic needs. However, I think there are many intangible (and priceless) goods in life, that can’t be bought, like the joy of spending time with family and friends, artistic and creative pursuits, and serving others, that make up a full and flourishing life. If taking a higher pay means having less time, this might mean less time available for these other important areas.

topicQuestion: Go for higher pay at expense of social life (For) or take lower pay to enjoy life (Against)?
text: Certainly, many things cannot be bought, but the option space increases significantly for someone with a higher income. Imagine a 10% cut in time but 40% growth in options. While the time spent with family inherently is the good, it is greatly supported by context of the time spent. Think back to your best memories with your family, many would probably recall holidays or activities, all of which a higher income can help. Also, many creative pursuits require income.

topicQuestion: Go for higher pay at expense of social life (For) or take lower pay to enjoy life (Against)?
text: I agree that there are some situations where a higher income would enable a higher quality of life overall, even at some expense to one’s social life. To move this forward — what do you think are the kinds of situations where making this tradeoff is worthwhile? For example, perhaps the duration of earning more with less time could be a factor, and one’s other responsibilities. I wonder if even just the experience of working an earning more could be formative (and not in a good way).

topicQuestion: Go for higher pay at expense of social life (For) or take lower pay to enjoy life (Against)?
text: This eventually boils down to quite an individual decision. However, I would say that some sort of measure of "diffusion" of social life could be used. If one finds themselves drained by certain social interactions or having very superficial connections/obligations, they should consider whether it is actually worthwhile to pare down on their social activities, in exchange for a higher-paying job. Finding a point where the benefits of their social life begins to saturate may make sense.

topicQuestion: Go for higher pay at expense of social life (For) or take lower pay to enjoy life (Against)?
text: That’s a great heuristic, and the scenario you describe is one where (to me) it would be worth considering being more involved at work. At the same time, I wonder if an alternative response to that scenario might not be to ’pare down’ relationships through the matrix of "what’s good for me", but to explore other ways to get engaged. Often, accepting more responsibilities at work — even with a higher pay — is a decision that might be hard to reverse; for one, because of lifestyle inflation.

topicQuestion: Go for higher pay at expense of social life (For) or take lower pay to enjoy life (Against)?
text: True, I have taken a rational, personal utility-maximizing argument so far. However, I think no matter what new ways you find to engage, both social life and an elevated lifestyle eventually saturate or reach diminishing marginal returns. And this is true for yourself and your loved ones. True that the decision may be hard to reverse, I’ll give that. However I would say that the alternative of more time does not guarantee a more fulfilling social life if your loved ones are not on the same page.

topicQuestion: Go for higher pay at expense of social life (For) or take lower pay to enjoy life (Against)?
text: I think that, while one can burn out at work, one can always fill one’s remaining time with a combination of personal challenges, hobbies, strengthening meaningful relationships, and otherwise. It’s true that one can definitely swing too far to one side, and be under-employed to the point of not providing for one’s family and other personal responsibilities.

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?
text: Yes, we should use ChatGPT for homework, at least in college. GenAI is the potentially the most consequential productivity multiplier we have seen in the third industrial revolution, and it will impact every field. It is imperative that students begin to adopt genAI in their workflows now, so they are prepared to use it in the workplace.

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?
text: I agree that generative AI is an essential skill for the future and is worth learning, but I don’t think the students should use it in homework because of that. I think the purpose of (some types) of homework is to develop students’ ability to think, and using ChatGPT (to devise an algorithm, to write a paper) would short-circuit that process — just like handing children a calculator would do the same for learning arithmetic.

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?
text: It is true that full dependence on ChatGPT could potentially short-circuit students ability to think. At the same time there is a counterbalancing force of enabling accessibility to a broader search space of ideas and rapid discovery. We ought to train students how to use these tools effectively and not mindlessly. Just like kids eventually pick up a calculator, students, especially college students should be made to learn effective, thoughtful ChatGPT use.

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?
text: Definitely, ChatGPT can let students access varied ideas, and more rapidly. And I agree they should be given the freedom to eventually experiment how to use them. But I think using ChatGPT (and other technologies) could not only short-circuit their learning to think, but also social and relational skills — as children who would have otherwise consulted teachers and peers for help now just ask an LLM. I think the risks outweigh the benefits, since it happens at a developmentally crucial stage.

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?
text: That is a fair point. Talking to ChatGPT certainly does not build social and relational skills. But again, this should not preclude its usage entirely. There is also the possibility of human-machine collaboration/co-evolution, which could involve not just man interfacing with machine, but groups of people collaborating with AI. This is a facet that could develop both the ways we relate with one another as well as machines at the same time.

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?
text:
I agree that human-machine collaboration is an essential for anyone entering the workforce today. I think that even then, there are benefits to limiting AI use for a time to learn effective collaboration between humans, before learning to collaborate with machines (instead of trying to do both).

I think one larger question that lies behind what we’ve discussed so far is, What is education for? I think that beyond preparing students for work, it has a large developmental and formative role too.

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?
text: If we are talking about collaboration skills for the workforce, we are (maybe unfortunately) in a time where the bulk of collaboration is not AI-free. So the bulk of training should be preparing for such a dynamic. It is true that education does have a role beyond preparation for work. But I don’t think this comes from the homeworks themselves. A lot of it may be through informal conversations, participation in student life activities, working in groups (which doesn’t go away).

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?
text:
I think it’s fair to say most collaboration will involve AI of some kind, and that it’s worth training for. Ijust think the place to do it is once people have entered the workforce, where they will be able to pick them up just as quickly.

And, even if students are disadvantaged in the workforce for lack of practice: I think it’s worthwhile, since using AI in homework irreversibly changes the tenor of many social interactions that happen only in school, e.g. asking for their friends’ help less.

text: If we want good answers from the AI, we need to give good prompts. Giving good prompts requires us to think, and such thinking processes involve using human judgment and discernment.
